figures.gloss: Iuppiter — sky-father and sovereign of the Roman state, 'best and greatest'; his name the literal cognate of Zeus-pater.

Sovereign goddess of the Roman state and of women's life-cycle, worshipped on the Capitol as Juno Regina alongside Jupiter and Minerva, as Juno Moneta at the mint beside her temple, and as Juno Lucina who watches over childbirth.
figures.facet: The triad's missing partner: Rome's own Capitoline theology names her beside Jupiter, and the corpus already carries her Etruscan donor Uni paired to Jupiter — this node is the Roman goddess that pairing was always pointing toward.
figures.provenance: Cicero, De Natura Deorum; Ovid, Fasti (on Juno Lucina and the Matronalia); Livy on the Capitoline cult; Beard, North & Price, Religions of Rome (1998).

figures.gloss: Second god of the Roman state after Jupiter, father of Romulus and Remus by Rhea Silvia; older than his classical war-god image, he was also protector of the field and flock, addressed in Cato's own prayer for the ritual purification of farmland (the suovetaurilia).
figures.facet: A god the record shows changing jobs: Cato's 2nd-century BCE prayer treats Mars as an agricultural guardian, while his later state cult (the months Martius and October framing the campaign season) is a war-god profile layered on top — no single 'war' archetype exists in this atlas to hold that later face without erasing the earlier one, so the tag is left off rather than forced.
figures.provenance: Cato, De Agri Cultura 141 (the Mars prayer); Livy on Romulus's parentage; Ovid, Fasti on March and October rites; Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: Third of the Capitoline triad, goddess of crafts, learned skill and strategy; patroness of guild artisans at the March Quinquatrus festival, later assimilated to Athena for her war aspect.
figures.facet: The corpus's tinia-uni-jupiter note already names her Etruscan donor Menrva as part of the Tinia-Uni-Menrva bequest; this node lands the Roman half of that documented triad-transfer.
figures.provenance: Ovid, Fasti (Quinquatrus); Cicero, De Natura Deorum; Beard, North & Price 1998 on the Capitoline cult's Etruscan origins.

figures.gloss: Goddess of the hearth, whose round temple in the Forum held Rome's undying flame, tended day and night by the six Vestal Virgins; the fire's extinguishing was read as a portent threatening the city's survival.
figures.facet: Rome's own theology made continuity itself the object of cult: Vesta is worshipped not as a personality with myths but as the fact of the fire staying lit — the state's survival made literally, materially dependent on an unbroken flame.
figures.provenance: Ovid, Fasti (on Vesta and the Vestals); Plutarch, Life of Numa (founding of the cult); Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: Two-faced god of doorways, beginnings and transitions, with no Greek equivalent; his temple gates stood open in war and were ceremonially closed in peace, and his name opens the year in January and headed every prayer sequence, invoked before all other gods.
figures.facet: The most distinctively Roman god in the whole pantheon precisely because he resists interpretatio graeca: every other Capitoline-tier deity has a Greek twin on record in this shard's edges, Janus alone does not, which is the plainest textual evidence of an authentically native theology beneath the borrowed Olympian veneer.
figures.provenance: Cicero, De Natura Deorum 2.67 (Janus invoked first, Vesta last, in the ritual prayer order); Ovid, Fasti Book 1; Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: God of sowing and of the lost golden age (the Saturnia regna), his temple in the Forum housing the state treasury; at his December Saturnalia, Rome's most famous festival, social order was ritually inverted — masters served slaves, gifts and license reigned.
figures.facet: The Roman echo of a wider Mediterranean pattern: like the Greek Cronos he was assimilated to, Saturn is a former sovereign displaced by Jupiter yet still invoked for the age of abundance he once presided over — the dethroned god remembered not as defeated but as the measure of what was lost.
figures.provenance: Macrobius, Saturnalia; Ovid, Fasti Book 1 (on Saturn's reign and the golden age); Beard, North & Price 1998 on the Saturnalia's social inversion.

figures.gloss: Goddess of charm and generative favor, elevated far beyond her archaic garden-cult origins as Venus Genetrix — the divine mother of Aeneas and thus, by claimed descent, of the Julian house — anchoring the theology of both Caesar and Augustus.
figures.facet: A goddess promoted by politics rather than myth: Venus's outsized centrality in imperial Rome traces to one dynasty's genealogical claim, not to any inherited cosmic function — the clearest case in this shard of state ideology remaking a deity's rank.
figures.provenance: Suetonius, Life of Julius Caesar (on the claimed Julian descent from Venus via Aeneas); Virgil, Aeneid; Beard, North & Price 1998 on Venus Genetrix's temple (46 BCE, Forum of Caesar).

figures.gloss: The wolf-suckled founder who killed his twin Remus at the city's new wall and was later, per tradition, taken up and identified with the god Quirinus — Rome's founding act deified, and the third member of the archaic triad Jupiter-Mars-Quirinus served by the three flamines maiores.
figures.facet: Rome's origin myth admits its own violence into the foundation: the city's beginning is inseparable from the fratricide, and the later apotheosis as Quirinus does not erase the killing — the founder-ancestor archetype here carries a settled crime at its root.
figures.provenance: Livy, Ab Urbe Condita Book 1; Plutarch, Life of Romulus; Ovid, Fasti Book 2 (Quirinalia); Beard, North & Price 1998 on the archaic triad and its flamines.

figures.gloss: The household guardians of hearth, stores and boundaries, fed daily at the lararium shrine of every Roman home; the state's own Penates were said to have been carried from Troy by Aeneas and were kept, alongside Vesta's flame, in her temple.
figures.facet: The daily substance the state cult's summit depended on: Rome's public religion of triads and temples rested on an ordinary domestic practice repeated in every household, the same gift-and-tend logic scaled from kitchen shrine to Forum temple.
figures.provenance: Cicero, De Legibus (on the Lares); Virgil, Aeneid (the Penates carried from Troy); Ovid, Fasti; Beard, North & Price 1998 on domestic cult.

figures.gloss: The impersonal divine will or assent — 'numen' — inhering in places, acts and gods alike, the pre-anthropomorphic register of Roman religiosity that scholars have long contrasted with Greek myth-fullness; a god's numen could be invoked and honored somewhat apart from any personal biography.
figures.facet: R. R. Marett's comparative-religion classic explicitly paired Roman numen with Melanesian mana as parallel cases of impersonal sacred power prior to personality — this shard's numen node and the vital-force archetype it carries were made for exactly that comparison.
figures.provenance: Georg Wissowa, Religion und Kultus der Römer (1912) on numen versus deus; R. R. Marett, The Threshold of Religion (1909) on the numen/mana comparison; Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: The guarded corpus of Greek oracular verses, consulted by decree of the Senate (through the duumviri, later decemviri, then quindecimviri sacris faciundis) only in times of crisis, whose prescriptions repeatedly imported foreign cults into the Roman state religion — most famously Magna Mater's transfer from Pessinus in 204 BCE.
figures.facet: Rome's own documented change-log for its pantheon: unlike myths that claim timeless origin, the Sibylline Books record, by name and by date, the mechanism through which the Roman gods' roster was deliberately expanded — religion changing itself by bureaucratic procedure.
figures.provenance: Livy 29.10-11 (the 204 BCE Magna Mater import by Sibylline prescription); Dionysius of Halicarnassus on the Books' acquisition and the sacred college; Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: The reading of the gods' assent chiefly in the flight and cries of birds, performed by the college of augurs; no assembly, election, or military campaign properly began without first taking the auspices, and an unfavorable sign could halt state business outright.
figures.facet: The clearest evidence of Rome's famous orthopraxy: correct belief mattered far less than correct procedure, and augury is the procedure — the college's job was not to interpret divine intent for its own sake but to certify whether an already-planned human action had permission to proceed.
figures.provenance: Cicero, De Divinatione; the Claudius Pulcher sacred-chickens episode before Drepana, 249 BCE (Livy epitome; Cicero, De Natura Deorum 2.7); Beard, North & Price 1998 on the augural college.

figures.gloss: God of fresh water and, after assimilation to Poseidon, of the sea and earthquakes; propitiated at the July Neptunalia during Rome's dry season, when temporary leaf-huts (umbrae) were built beside the Tiber against the heat.
figures.facet: A god whose portfolio visibly grew: the earliest Neptunalia addresses drought and fresh water, not the ocean — the sea-god profile is the later Greek layer, a textbook case of interpretatio graeca reshaping a native Italic deity's actual function.
figures.provenance: Varro, De Lingua Latina (on the Neptunalia and umbrae); Beard, North & Price 1998 on Neptune's expanded, Hellenized profile.

figures.gloss: God of trade, profit and messages, whose temple near the Circus Maximus (dedicated 495 BCE) served Rome's guild of merchants (mercatores); merged with Hermes he also inherited the Greek god's office of guide of souls to the underworld.
figures.facet: The clearest case of a Roman god's whole function set imported wholesale with his Greek name-partner: Mercury's psychopomp role has no independent Italic root — it arrives strictly as part of the Hermes package, unlike Jupiter or Mars who had native profiles before any Greek overlay.
figures.provenance: Livy 2.21 (the 495 BCE temple dedication); Ovid, Fasti 5 (Mercuralia); Beard, North & Price 1998 on Mercury's merchant cult and Hellenization.

figures.gloss: Grain-goddess of the Aventine Triad with Liber and Libera, celebrated at the April Cerealia; her temple, funded from war spoils and administered by plebeian aediles, became a center of plebeian political and religious life against the patrician Capitol.
figures.facet: A cult explicitly imported by rite, not just by name: after the famine of 496 BCE the Sibylline Books prescribed introducing Demeter-Persephone-Dionysus worship 'graeco ritu' — 'in the Greek manner' — Rome's own sources naming the transplant as a transplant.
figures.provenance: Livy 3.55 on the Aventine Triad and its plebeian associations; Cicero, In Verrem 2.4 on the Sicilian Ceres cult imported 'graeco ritu'; Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: Huntress goddess of the wild, worshipped at her woodland shrine at Lake Nemi (where the priest-king, the Rex Nemorensis, held office only by killing his predecessor) and at her Aventine temple, traditionally founded by Servius Tullius as a Latin-League sanctuary and refuge for runaway slaves.
figures.facet: The Nemi priesthood's murderous succession rule made this node the opening case study of comparative religion itself — Frazer built The Golden Bough (1890) around the question of why Diana's priest must be his predecessor's killer.
figures.provenance: Strabo, Geography 5.3.12 (Nemi and the Rex Nemorensis); Livy 1.45 (the Aventine temple and asylum tradition); J. G. Frazer, The Golden Bough (1890, opening chapters).

figures.gloss: God of destructive and purifying fire and of the forge, honored at the August Volcanalia with bonfires and, distinctively, offerings of live fish thrown into flame; his ancient shrine, the Volcanal, stood in the Forum itself, older than most of the temples around it.
figures.facet: A fire-god split cleanly by function from Vesta: where Vesta's flame must never go out, Vulcan governs fire's destructive and purifying face — the same element personified twice, into opposite theological jobs, in the same pantheon.
figures.provenance: Varro, De Lingua Latina; Ovid, Fasti (Volcanalia offerings); Beard, North & Price 1998 on the Volcanal and its antiquity.

figures.gloss: Native Italic god of wine, fertility and libertas, merged with the Greek Dionysus-Bacchus; his ecstatic cult was investigated and drastically curtailed by the Senate after the 186 BCE Bacchanalia scandal, recorded in Livy and preserved verbatim on the surviving bronze Senatus Consultum de Bacchanalibus.
figures.facet: One of history's best-documented state suppressions of a religious cult: the inscription itself survives, naming exactly which nocturnal rites were banned and under what penalty — a rare case where the crackdown's own bureaucratic paper trail outlived the scandal it answered.
figures.provenance: Livy 39.8-19 (the Bacchanalia affair); the Senatus Consultum de Bacchanalibus (CIL I2 581), the surviving bronze inscription of 186 BCE; Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: Goddess of luck and turning chance, consulted above all at her oracle of Fortuna Primigenia in Praeneste, where a boy drew inscribed wooden lots (sortes) to answer petitioners; also honored as Fortuna Redux for safe return from journeys and, per the Coriolanus legend, as Fortuna Muliebris.
figures.facet: The deliberate non-fit flagged rather than forced: this atlas's cosmic-order joint names fixed, law-like allotment (Rta, Ma'at, Laima's lemtis), while Fortuna is precisely the opposite valence — contingency and reversible luck — so no archetype tag is applied here rather than stretching cosmic-order to cover its inverse.
figures.provenance: Cicero, De Divinatione (on the Praeneste sortes); Livy 2.40 (Fortuna Muliebris and Coriolanus); Beard, North & Price 1998.

figures.gloss: The personal indwelling tutelary spirit born with every man (a woman's counterpart being her Juno), honored at his birthday and on the household altar; under the Principate, subjects swore oaths by the Genius of the living emperor as a way of venerating him without directly declaring him a god.
figures.facet: Distinguished here from Numen on purpose: Numen names divine power in general and impersonal terms, while Genius is that same register of sacred force narrowed to a single person's own indwelling spirit — the cosmic and the personal registers of one Roman idea, kept as two nodes rather than merged.
figures.provenance: Horace, Epistles 2.2 (on the Genius and personal fate); the imperial oath 'by the Genius of Augustus/the emperor' discussed in Beard, North & Price 1998 as the mechanism of the living-ruler cult.

figures.gloss: Chief of the College of Pontiffs, overseeing the state calendar, religious law, and the Vestal Virgins; from Augustus onward every emperor held the title permanently, folding Rome's highest priesthood into the office of the ruler himself.
figures.facet: The institutional hinge the roster otherwise lacked: this office is what actually ran Roman public religion day to day — setting the calendar, judging religious disputes, disciplining the Vestals — the administrative reality behind every deity and rite this shard names.
figures.provenance: Livy on the pontifical college's functions; Suetonius, Life of Augustus 31 (assuming the office, 12 BCE); Beard, North & Price 1998 on the pontifex maximus's later fusion with the emperorship.

edges.note: Iu-piter < *Dyēus-ph₂tēr — 'Sky-father' preserved almost intact in the compound.
Siblings, not borrowings: both descend straight from *Dyēus-ph₂tēr.
Dievas and Iuppiter (Dieus-pater) share the *Dyeus root by regular sound-law within Indo-European.
GOLD SEAM: Worshipped across the empire as Jupiter Ammon, blending the supreme Roman sovereign with the desert oracle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The astrological planets are historically named for, and imbued with the exact temperaments of, the Roman pantheon.
The Etruscans' own equation: Tinia is the model and donor for Roman Jupiter, the Capitoline triad (Tinia-Uni-Menrva → Jupiter-Juno-Minerva) being the most direct Etruscan bequest to Rome.
Consort-pair within the Etruscan triad, mirroring the Tinia-Uni sovereignty couple at the head of the pantheon.
The succession theme reaches Rome through the Greek inheritance — Jupiter as heir of the same dethroning-the-elder pattern Hesiod drew from the Kumarbi cycle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both derive from the same root of the daytime sky, though Jupiter evolved into a state-builder while Deipaturos remained a tribal elemental force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Attested syncretism as 'Iuppiter Reve', matching the supreme Roman sky god with the indigenous sovereign of the peaks.
GOLD SEAM: Sol Invictus effectively supplanted Jupiter as the head of the Roman state pantheon in the late 3rd century, absorbing the role of the supreme protector of the state.
GOLD SEAM: Heavily syncretized in the Roman period as Jupiter Sabazius, merging the ultimate sky-father with the ecstatic mystery cult.

Same planet under different pantheons' naming, but the theological content differs (Harranian astral intellect vs. Roman sky-sovereign) — kept at the structural-analogy tier rather than claimed as continuous descent.
The Etruscans' own equation, already carried in this corpus for Jupiter: Uni is the donor for Juno within the Tinia-Uni-Menrva triad Rome inherited as Jupiter-Juno-Minerva.
after Carthage's fall Roman Africa continued Tanit's cult under the name Juno Caelestis, a documented provincial assimilation of the Punic 'Face of Baal' into the Roman state goddess.
Etruscan Menrva is Minerva's attested donor within the same Tinia-Uni-Menrva triad already linked to Jupiter in this corpus — completing the triad-transfer on the Roman side.
Interpretatio graeca: Vulcan's forge-fire and Hephaestus's smith-craft were equated wholesale, each pantheon's fire-god read as the other's twin.
Interpretatio graeca equation bringing Mercury the god of trade and messages his full Hermes function set, psychopomp role included.

Ceres's Aventine cult was explicitly instituted 'graeco ritu' after the 496 BCE famine, importing Demeter-Persephone-Dionysus worship as Ceres-Libera-Liber by Sibylline prescription — a documented rite-transplant, not mere name equation.
Interpretatio graeca merging Diana with Artemis as huntress-goddess of the wild, sharing the bow, the untamed grove, and the later lunar/triple extension.

The modern Stregheria revival's Diana claims descent from this Roman goddess via Charles Leland's Aradia (1899) — a documented revival-of claim resting on a single contested folklore source, not proven cultic continuity.

Interpretatio graeca merging native Liber Pater with Dionysus-Bacchus; the merged cult's 186 BCE suppression is Rome's best-documented religious crackdown, preserved on the surviving bronze Senatus Consultum de Bacchanalibus.
Intra-tradition: Mars fathers the twins by Rhea Silvia, and Romulus's deification as Quirinus completes the archaic Jupiter-Mars-Quirinus triad served by Rome's three senior flamens.

Intra-tradition: Augustan state theology paired them as Rome's two divine ancestors — Venus Genetrix, mother of Aeneas and the Julii, and Mars, father of Romulus — each anchoring a founding bloodline of the city and its ruling dynasty.

Intra-tradition: Cicero records the fixed prayer order 'Janus first, Vesta last' — the two gods framing every ritual invocation between them, beginning and hearth-continuity bracketing the whole pantheon.
Intra-tradition: the same hearth-and-household logic at two scales — Vesta's undying public flame in the Forum and the Lares/Penates fed daily at every private lararium.
Intra-tradition: Genius particularizes to a single person what Numen names as impersonal divine assent in general — the same Roman concept of indwelling sacred power at personal and cosmic scale.
The Sibylline Books' 204 BCE prescription is the documented mechanism behind Magna Mater's import from Pessinus — the state's own change-log naming the object of its most famous cult transplant.

R. R. Marett's foundational comparative-religion pairing: numen and mana as parallel cases of impersonal sacred power theorized prior to, or beneath, personal godhood — a scholarly analogy, not a historical contact.
Cognate siblings from *Dyēus ph₂tḗr: Jupiter remained the active sovereign of the Roman state cult while Vedic Dyaus faded to a near-empty name — the same inherited Sky Father, opposite trajectories.

dossier.essence: The polytheistic state and civic religion of ancient Rome and its empire, from the archaic city (traditionally founded 753 BCE) through the Imperial period until public pagan cult was legally suppressed under Theodosius I (measures of 380–392 CE). It centered on maintaining the pax deorum — right relations between the community and its gods — through correct ritual, vow, and sacrifice. Religion was bound tightly to the political order, and correct performance mattered more than private belief.
dossier.cosmos: A vast, open pantheon headed by the Capitoline triad of Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva, with gods of state, place, and function and a divine power (numen) immanent in hearth, boundary, spring, and household (the Lares and Penates). Rome freely absorbed and identified foreign gods (interpretatio Romana), taking in Greek myth, Etruscan divinatory science, and later Eastern mystery cults (Cybele, Isis, Mithras) and the imperial cult of deified emperors. It was little concerned with systematic cosmology or a doctrinal afterlife: ideas of the dead (the Manes, the underworld) coexisted with philosophical and mystery-cult alternatives.
dossier.practice: Public religion was largely non-congregational — animal sacrifice, prayer, and vow performed for the community by magistrate-priests and colleges: the pontifices under the pontifex maximus, the augurs who read the auspices, the Vestal Virgins guarding the sacred fire, and the haruspices reading entrails in the Etruscan discipline. The Sibylline Books were consulted in crisis. Orthopraxy — exact performance — outweighed belief, and domestic cult honored the household gods and ancestors.
dossier.canon: No revealed scripture: religion rested on ritual tradition (the mos maiorum), priestly calendars and records (the fasti), and consulted collections such as the Sibylline Books. It is known largely through literary sources — Ovid's 'Fasti,' Livy, Cicero's 'On the Nature of the Gods' and 'On Divination,' and Varro's largely lost 'Antiquitates.'
dossier.reflects: Religion as the contractual upkeep of civic order — correct ritual reciprocity with the gods, fused to the state, where orthopraxy and the pax deorum outweigh belief or personal salvation.
sources: Mary Beard, John North, and Simon Price, 'Religions of Rome' (1998)
Robert Turcan, 'The Gods of Ancient Rome' (2000)
